contest.title: IAEPC Preliminary Contest (Codeforces Round 999, Div. 1 + Div. 2)

sources.notes: Read the operation, all examples, and constraints; repeated use of one magic type is idempotent.
Official editorial supplies the highest-differing-bit exchange proof of discrete convexity and the global top-k reduction.
Cross-checked the full subset-by-popcount implementation, marginal-difference construction, and sorting approach.
Independent write-up confirms subset-mask preprocessing, decreasing marginal gains, and the optional nth_element optimization.

annotation.summary: Apply at most k operations of the form a_i <- a_i & b_j and minimize the final sum of a.
annotation.constraints: There are at most 10000 test cases, sum(n) <= 100000, 1 <= m <= 10, 0 <= k <= nm, and all values are below 2^30. Enumerating 2^m magic subsets per a_i is feasible; the sum and answer require 64-bit integers.
annotation.core_idea: For a fixed value x, repeated magic types are useless, so enumerate subsets S of the m types. Let f_x(S) be x AND all b_j in S, and let g_x(c)=min_{|S|=c} f_x(S). The sequence g_x is discretely convex. To prove it, take optimal subsets S and T of sizes c-1 and c+1 and let w be the highest bit where f_x(S) and f_x(T) differ. That bit is 1 in f_x(S) and 0 in f_x(T), so some y in T\S clears it. For U=S union {y}, f_x(S)-f_x(U) >= 2^w, while f_x(U) and f_x(T) agree through bit w and therefore f_x(U)-f_x(T) < 2^w. Since g_x(c) <= f_x(U), this gives 2g_x(c) <= g_x(c-1)+g_x(c+1). Hence the reductions d_x(c)=g_x(c-1)-g_x(c) are non-increasing. Compute every g_{a_i}(c) by grouping subset masks by popcount, collect all nm marginal reductions, and take the k largest. The decreasing order within each element means any chosen later reduction can be exchanged for an unchosen earlier one, so a prefix-closed and therefore realizable selection always exists.
annotation.complexity: O(m*2^m + n*2^m + nm*log(nm)) time and O(2^m + nm) memory with sorting; nth_element reduces the final selection to expected O(nm) time.
tricks: Replace an operation sequence on one element by a subset because bitwise AND is commutative and idempotent.
Group all magic subsets by popcount to obtain the best residual value for every exact operation count.
Prove diminishing marginal reductions with a highest-differing-bit exchange.
Merge all decreasing marginal-gain chains by globally selecting the k largest gains.

solution_variants.name: subset-cardinality-convex-greedy
solution_variants.summary: Precompute the AND of every magic subset, enumerate those masks for each a_i to obtain minimum residuals by subset size, difference the resulting convex sequence into non-increasing reductions, and select the globally largest k reductions using sorting or nth_element.
solution_variants.complexity: O(m*2^m + n*2^m + nm*log(nm)) time and O(2^m + nm) memory with full sorting

tags.evidence: Every subset of the at most ten magic types is encoded by a mask, and its popcount and cumulative AND determine one candidate for g_i(c).
After proving each element's marginal reductions are non-increasing, the optimum is obtained by greedily selecting the k largest reductions globally.
The highest-differing-bit lemma proves discrete convexity of g_i(c), equivalently that successive reductions g_i(c-1)-g_i(c) are non-increasing.
Differencing the best exact-count values converts each element's allocation choices into a chain of marginal gains that can be merged globally.
The small bound m <= 10 permits exhaustive enumeration of all 2^m magic subsets for every a_i.
A standard implementation sorts the nm marginal reductions and subtracts the largest k from the initial sum; nth_element is an equivalent optimization.
